text_body: I have a small cat at home. Her name is Mimi. She has very soft white fur. She likes to sleep on my bed at night. I give her food every day. She plays with a little red ball. I love my cat so much. Mimi is my very best friend.

Spring is here. The snow is gone. Green leaves come out on the trees. Little flowers open in the garden. They are pink, yellow, and white. Birds sing in the morning. The air is warm and soft. I love spring.

Summer is hot and bright. The sun shines all day long. We go to the beach and the pool. We eat cold ice cream. Green trees are full of leaves. We play outside until night. Summer is warm and fun. I love summer days.

At night, the stars come out. They shine like tiny lights. The sky is dark and deep. There are so many stars. Some are big, and some are small. I look up and make a wish. The stars are quiet and beautiful. I love the night sky.

You can make a sandwich at home. First get two slices of bread. Put them on a plate. Next put some butter on one slice. Then add cheese and ham on top. Put the other slice of bread over it. Now cut it in half. Your sandwich is ready to eat.

Colors are all around us. The sky is blue. Grass is green. Apples can be red or green. The sun is yellow and very bright. Clouds are white and soft. At night the sky turns dark. We see so many colors every day. Colors make our world a pretty place.

I have a small garden. Red and yellow flowers grow there. Green leaves dance in the wind. A small bee comes to say hello. Birds sing in the morning sun. I water my flowers every day. My garden is full of life. I love my little garden.

It is snowing today! White snow falls from the sky. It covers the trees and the road. The world is soft and white. We make a big snowman. We give it round eyes and a long nose. Our hands are cold, but we laugh. What a happy snow day!

Wash your hands before you eat. First, turn on the water. Next, put soap on your hands. Rub your hands together. Rub for a long time. Then wash the soap away. Last, dry your hands with a towel. Now your hands are clean!

In summer, the weather is hot. The sun is strong. We wear short clothes. We drink cold water. In winter, the weather is cold. We wear warm coats and hats. We drink hot tea. Hot and cold come with the seasons.

Dogs are very popular pets. They come in many sizes and colors. Some dogs are big and some are small. Dogs can run and jump very well. They wag their tails when they are happy. Dogs need food and water and love. A dog is a very good friend to have.

Bees are small insects. They have wings, so they can fly. Bees like flowers. They go from flower to flower. Bees make honey. Honey is sweet and yellow. We eat honey. Bees are small, but they help us very much.

Rain comes from clouds. Clouds are in the sky. When it rains, water falls down. The rain makes the ground wet. Plants drink the rain. The rain helps them grow. After the rain, we can see a rainbow. Rain is good for the Earth.

Water is very important. All living things need water. We drink water every day. Plants drink water to grow. Fish live in water. Water can be a river, a lake, or the sea. We must keep our water clean. Water gives life to the Earth.

Cats are soft, small animals. Many people keep cats as pets. A cat has four legs and a long tail. Cats like to sleep in the sun. They like to play with balls. Cats say 'meow.' They clean their fur with their tongue. Cats are quiet and cute.

At night, I look at the moon. The moon is round and white. It shines in the dark sky. It looks like a big white plate. Little stars are around it. The moon is quiet and calm. 'Good night, moon,' I say. Then I close my eyes.

Today was a fun day at school. My teacher read us a story about a bear. The bear lived in a big green forest. We drew pictures of the bear. I used brown and green colors. Then we went outside to the playground. I played on the slide with my friend Jina. We ran very fast together. After that we ate our lunch. I had rice and soup. Jina had bread and milk. We talked about our favorite animals. I like dogs and Jina likes rabbits. It was a very happy day. I want to go to school again tomorrow.

We should help our friends. A good friend is kind. When a friend is sad, we can talk to them. When a friend falls, we can help them up. When a friend needs help, we say, 'I will help you.' Friends help each other. That is what friends are for.

Children should sleep early. Sleep is good for us. When we sleep, our body grows. We feel happy in the morning. If we sleep late, we feel tired. We cannot play well. We cannot study well. So go to bed early. Sleep well and feel great!

Trees are tall plants. They have a trunk, branches, and leaves. Trees grow very slowly. They give us clean air. Birds make nests in trees. Some trees give us fruit, like apples. In autumn, the leaves fall down. Trees are important for the Earth.

The moon sits high in the dark night sky. It glows like a big white lamp. The little stars dance around it. The moon watches over sleeping children below. The whole world is quiet and soft. The night is so calm and still. The moon smiles down at us.
I have a dog. His name is Max. Max is brown and white. He is small and fast. Every morning, Max runs to my bed. He wants to play. I love my dog Max. He is my best friend.

Let's plant a seed. First, put soil in a small pot. Next, make a little hole in the soil. Put the seed in the hole. Then cover it with soil. Now give it some water. Put the pot near the window. Wait a few days. Soon a green plant will grow!

We must brush our teeth every day. First, put toothpaste on the brush. Next, brush the top teeth up and down. Then brush the bottom teeth, too. Brush the front and the back. Brush for two minutes. Last, rinse your mouth with water. Now your teeth are clean!
I think dogs are the best pets. Dogs are kind and friendly. They love to play with us. A dog can run and catch a ball. Dogs make us happy. They are good friends. This is why I like dogs the most.

Vegetables are good for us. We should eat them every day. Carrots, beans, and spinach are vegetables. They make our body strong. They help us grow tall. Some children do not like vegetables. But vegetables keep us healthy. So let's eat our vegetables and grow strong!

The Earth turns around every day. When our side faces the sun, it is day. The sky is bright. When our side turns away from the sun, it is night. The sky is dark. The Earth turns slowly. So we have day and night every day.

Birds can fly in the sky. They have two wings. They move their wings up and down. Birds have light bodies. Their bones are not heavy. They have soft feathers, too. The feathers help them fly. So birds can fly high in the sky.

We wear clothes every day. Clothes keep our body warm. In winter, we wear thick coats. In summer, we wear light shirts. Clothes also keep us clean and safe. Some clothes are for play. Some clothes are for school. We wear different clothes for different days.
Today I got new shoes. They are red and white. They are very clean. I put them on and run outside. I run fast in my new shoes. 'Look at me!' I say to my mom. She smiles. I love my new red shoes.

It is easy to draw a cat. First, draw a big circle for the head. Next, draw two small triangles for the ears. Then draw two eyes and a little nose. Add some lines for the whiskers. Draw a round body and a long tail. Now color your cat. You drew a cat!

Drawing a face is easy and fun. First get a paper and a pencil. Draw a big circle for the head. Next draw two small circles for the eyes. Put them near the top of the face. Then draw a little round nose below the eyes. Now draw a curved line for the mouth. You can make it smile or frown. Add two small ears on the sides of the head. Draw some lines on top for hair. You can color the face with your crayons. Give it blue eyes or brown eyes. Now your happy face is all done.

I love to read books. Books are fun and full of stories. When I read, I learn new things. I can go to many places in a book. I can meet dragons, kings, and animals. Books make me smart and happy. I think everyone should read books every day.

We should be kind to animals. Animals feel happy and sad, just like us. We can give food to a hungry cat. We can give water to a thirsty dog. We should not hurt small animals. We should love them. Be kind to all animals, big and small.

We should recycle. Recycling is good for the Earth. We can recycle paper, cans, and bottles. We put them in the right box. Then they become new things. Recycling keeps our world clean. It helps animals and plants, too. Let's all recycle every day!

Do you drink milk? Milk comes from cows. Cows live on farms. They eat grass all day. Farmers get milk from the cows. Then trucks take the milk to stores. We buy the milk and drink it. Milk is white and good for us. It makes our bones strong.

Plants have many parts. Each part has a job to do. The roots are under the ground. They take in water from the soil. The stem is the tall part of the plant. It holds the plant up straight. Water moves up through the stem. The leaves are green and flat. They use sunlight to make food for the plant. The flower is the pretty part. It can be many colors like red or pink. Some plants grow fruit. The fruit has seeds inside it. Seeds can grow into new plants. Plants need sun and water and soil to live.

There are four seasons in a year. In spring, flowers bloom. In summer, the weather is hot. In autumn, leaves turn red and yellow. In winter, it is cold and white. Each season is different. Each season is special. I like all four seasons.

Let's make a sandwich. First, take two pieces of bread. Next, put butter on the bread. Then add some cheese and ham. You can add a leaf of lettuce, too. Put the two pieces together. Cut the sandwich in half. Now your sandwich is ready to eat. Enjoy!

Today is my birthday! I am eight years old now. My mom made a big cake. It has eight candles on top. My friends came to my house. We played fun games together. I opened my presents. I got a book and a toy car. I made a wish and blew out the candles. It was the best day!

On a hot summer day, we went to the beach. The sand was soft and warm. The sea was big and blue. I made a sandcastle with my dad. My sister found pretty shells. We jumped in the cool water. We ate watermelon for lunch. The beach was so much fun. I want to go again!

Today is my birthday. I am seven years old. Mom made a big cake for me. The cake has white cream and red strawberries on top. It looks so pretty. My friends come to my house after school. Jina brings me a pink hair band. Siwoo gives me a toy car. Hana gives me a coloring book. I am so happy. We play games in the living room. First we play hide and seek. I hide behind the sofa. Siwoo finds me very fast. Then we play musical chairs. Hana wins the game. She is very quick. Mom brings the cake to the table. Everyone sings the birthday song for me. I close my eyes and make a wish. I blow out the candles. We eat cake and drink juice. The cake is sweet and soft. After that we go to the park. We run around and play on the swings. The sun is warm and the sky is blue. My friends go home before dinner. I wave goodbye to them. Mom asks me about my day. I tell her it was the best day ever. I hug my mom and say thank you. I go to bed with a big smile.

Our school should be clean. A clean school is a nice place. When the school is clean, we feel happy. We can study well. We can play well. It is easy to keep our school clean. We can put trash in the trash can. We can clean our desks. We can pick up paper on the floor. Everyone can help. Let's all work together. Let's keep our school clean and bright!

Kimbap is a popular Korean food. Many children like it. Kimbap is rice and vegetables in black seaweed. To make kimbap, we put rice on the seaweed. Then we add carrots, eggs, and ham. We roll it up tight. Next, we cut it into small pieces. Each piece looks like a little wheel. Kimbap is easy to carry. We often eat it on a picnic. Kimbap is healthy and very tasty. I love to eat kimbap with my family.

My favorite place is under the big tree in my yard. The tree is very old and very tall. Its leaves are green in summer. They turn red and yellow in fall. I sit under the tree and read my books. The wind blows softly through the branches. It sounds like the tree is singing to me. Birds come and sit on the branches too. Sometimes a squirrel runs up the trunk. I feel happy and safe under my tree. When I am sad I go to the tree. It always makes me feel better. I think the tree is my quiet friend.

The sun is a big, bright star. It is very far from us. The sun gives us light. It gives us heat, too. In the morning, the sun comes up. The sky is bright. At night, the sun goes down. The sky is dark. Plants need the sun to grow. People need the sun to feel warm. We cannot live without the sun. The sun is very important for the Earth.

It is fun to make a paper airplane. First, take a piece of paper. Next, fold the paper in the middle. Then open it again. Fold the two top corners to the middle line. After that, fold the paper in half again. Now fold the two sides down to make wings. Your paper airplane is ready! Hold it gently and throw it. Watch your airplane fly across the room. Try again and make it fly far!

Every night, we go to sleep. Sleep is very important for our body. When we sleep, our body rests. Our brain rests, too. Children need a lot of sleep to grow. Sleep helps us feel happy and strong. If we do not sleep enough, we feel tired. We cannot think well. It is hard to play and learn. So we must sleep every night. Go to bed early and sleep well. Then you will feel good in the morning.

On Sunday, my family went to the zoo. The sun was warm and bright. First, we saw the big elephants. They had long noses and big ears. Next, we looked at the monkeys. They jumped from tree to tree. I laughed and laughed. Then we ate lunch on the grass. I had a rice ball and an apple. In the afternoon, we saw a lion. It was sleeping in the sun. I had a wonderful day at the zoo.

Mina has a little cat. Its name is Nabi. Nabi is white and soft. One day, Nabi did not come home. Mina was very sad. She looked under the bed. She looked behind the door. She called, 'Nabi! Nabi!' Then she heard a small sound. It came from the box in the kitchen. Mina looked inside. Nabi was sleeping in the box! Mina was so happy. She hugged her cat. 'I found you!' she said with a big smile.

Sam lost his house key on the way home from school. He looked in his bag and his pockets, but it was not there. He felt worried because Mom was not home yet. Then he found it under a big leaf near the door.

Spring brings flowers and baby birds. Summer fills the days with sunshine and play. Fall paints the leaves in red and gold. Winter covers the world in soft white snow. Each season is different, but every one is beautiful in its own special way.

topic: Why Is Korean Literature Getting Global Attention?
text_body:
"Did you hear the news?" Jisoo ran into the classroom, waving her phone. "Han Kang won the Nobel Prize in Literature!" Mina’s eyes widened. "Seriously? That’s amazing! I read The Vegetarian last year. It was so unique."

"I know! She’s the first Korean author to win the prize," Jisoo said proudly. "People all over the world are talking about Korean literature now." Mina smiled. "I guess K-literature is joining K-pop and K-dramas in the spotlight."

"Exactly! Korean stories are deep and emotional. They talk about things like family, identity, and pain," Jisoo explained. "More books are being translated into English so that international readers can enjoy them too." "I want to read more Korean novels in English," Mina said. "It feels good to see our culture being recognized." Jisoo nodded. "Same here. It makes me proud to be Korean."

topic: Why Is Retro Style Cool Again?
text_body:
Hana: Hey, is that a cassette player?

Jisoo: Yup! It was my dad’s. I found it in the closet, and it still works!

Hana: That’s so cool. Retro stuff is everywhere lately. I’ve even seen people wearing 90s windbreakers.

Jisoo: I know. I’ve been listening to old K-pop songs, and some of them sound better than the new ones.

Hana: I’ve started wearing my mom’s vintage jeans. At first, I thought people would laugh, but they actually said I looked trendy.

Jisoo: If we had worn this stuff a few years ago, people might’ve thought we were weird. Now it’s in style!

Hana: Retro just has a different vibe. It feels more fun and original.

Jisoo: Totally. I love mixing old and new. It’s like bringing history back—with a twist.

Making an origami cup is easy and fun. First, get a square piece of paper. Fold it in half to make a triangle. Then fold each corner to the opposite side. Finally, open the top part and push it down. Now you have a little paper cup!

topic: American English vs. British English: What’s the Difference?
text_body:
Have you ever seen someone write "color" and someone else writes "colour"? Both are correct—just from different kinds of English! American English is used in the U.S., while British English is used in the U.K.

Some words look or sound different. Americans say "elevator," but the British say "lift." In the U.S., people wear "sneakers," while in the U.K., they wear "trainers." Even spelling changes! Americans write "center," but British people write "centre." Pronunciation can be different, too. In British English, the "r" sound is often softer or silent at the end of words. In American English, it’s stronger.

Sometimes, these differences can be confusing, but don’t worry—both are correct! The most important thing is to understand and be understood. So whether you watch an American movie or read a British book, you still learn English—which really matters.

topic: How Can Minji’s Late-Night Shopping Habit Be Fixed?
text_body:
It was 11:45 p.m., and Minji was still scrolling through an online shopping app. "Only five left in stock!" the message said. Her finger hovered over the buy button. "Do I really need this?" she asked herself. Still, she clicked "Order."

The following day, she barely remembered what she had bought. Packages kept arriving—small things like keychains, socks, and notebooks. Her room was filling up, but she didn’t feel excited anymore. One day, her dad asked, "How many of these things do you actually use?" Minji looked around and didn’t know what to say. She realized she had been shopping out of boredom, not a necessity.

After that, she made a simple rule: no shopping after 9 p.m. At first, it was hard, but soon, she felt more in control. Online shopping was still fun—but now, she was the one making the decisions, not the app.

topic: What Brings Yuna’s Break from Social Media?
text_body:
Yuna used to check her phone every few minutes. She posted selfies, watched videos, and counted how many likes she got. When her friends didn’t comment, she felt a little disappointed. Maybe I’m not interesting enough, she thought.

One day, her phone broke. At first, she panicked. "How will I know what’s happening?" she cried. But after a few days, something changed. She read a book for pleasure. She took a walk without taking pictures. She even communicated more with her family.

When her phone was fixed, she didn’t rush to post. She had realized something important. "I like social media," she said, "But I don’t need it to be happy." Yuna still uses her apps, but not all the time. She has found a better balance, and that has made her smile more than any number of likes.

topic: Digital Detox: Can You Live Without Your Phone?
text_body:
Imagine a whole day without your phone—no chatting, no games, no videos—just real life. Sounds difficult, right? But some people say a digital detox is the best way to feel sound again.

Many students check their phones every few minutes. I’ve done it too. But when I tried one day without my phone, I was surprised. I had more time to communicate with my family, go outside, and even finish my homework faster. It felt good!

Of course, phones are useful. We need them for school, music, and staying in touch. But if we use them too much, we might miss the world around us. You might think, "I can’t live without it!" But you never know—maybe you can. Try a short digital detox. You might enjoy it more than you expect.

topic: How Did K-Pop Become Part of My Life and Everyone Else’s?
text_body:
I still remember the first time I heard K-pop. It was BTS’s "Dynamite," and I couldn’t stop dancing. Since then, I’ve watched music videos, learned dances, and even followed fan accounts from other countries. I never expected people in Brazil, Germany, or the U.S. to love K-pop as much as I do—but they really do!

One time, I joined a live concert stream online. Fans from all over the world were cheering in the chat. If you had seen it, you would’ve felt the energy too. Some even typed messages in Korean!

K-pop isn’t just about music. It’s the style, the stories, the teamwork. The more I learn, the more I admire the effort idols put in. They train so hard before they even debut. I smile when I hear K-pop playing in a store or a cafe. It feels like a part of home is everywhere.

topic: Farming Without Dirt: Welcome to the Future
text_body:
When our science class visited a smart farm, I expected fields and mud. But instead, we stepped into a bright, white building that looked more like a hospital. No soil. No tractors. Just rows of green plants growing under pink and purple lights.

A scientist in a lab coat smiled and said, "This is vertical farming. These plants grow with LED lights, water mist, and AI sensors." I looked up and saw tiny robots moving along tracks, checking the leaves. He showed us a screen. "This system knows exactly when each plant needs light, water, or air. No waste, no chemicals."

I was amazed. It didn’t smell like a farm. But everything was fresh, clean, and growing fast. On the way back, I thought, maybe farms won’t look like farms anymore—but they’ll still grow the food we eat every day.

topic: Why Do We Love Minecraft and Roblox So Much?
text_body:
One weekend, I spent hours building a huge underwater house in Minecraft. I didn’t follow a guide—I just used my imagination. When I showed it to my friend, he said, "You should see what I made in Roblox!"

We started sharing our creations and even played games he designed himself. I was amazed. In Roblox, players can be game developers, not just players. In Minecraft, you can create entire worlds from blocks. There are no limits—just ideas.

What I love most is that we can play together. Sometimes, we build, explore, and sometimes laugh at our mistakes. These games aren’t just fun. They let us be creative, think differently, and work as a team. That’s why I keep going back. Every time I play, it feels like I’m building a new story.

topic: What Kind of Impact Do Electric Cars Have on the Environment?
text_body:
Last weekend, I saw my uncle’s new electric car for the first time."Isn’t it cool?" he said, smiling as the car started without a sound. I had never been in an electric vehicle before, so I was pretty excited. I noticed how quiet and smooth it was as we drove through the neighborhood.

"Electric cars are cleaner, right?" I asked. He nodded. "They don’t pollute the air when you drive them. And they can produce 50% fewer emissions over time than gas cars." But then he added, "Still, the batteries take a lot of energy to make. And if we use coal-powered electricity, they’re not so green after all."

That surprised me. I thought electric cars were always clean. "If we use more solar or wind energy," he said, "They could really help the planet." I looked out the window and thought, Maybe this quiet car could help us drive toward a cleaner future.

topic: "That’s So Fire!" – The Fun of Slang Words
text_body:
I was playing a game online when one of the players said, "That move was sus." I had no idea what that meant, so I just stayed quiet. Later, I asked my friend Jisoo, and she laughed. "Sus means suspicious," she explained. "It’s slang—everyone says it!"

After that, I started noticing slang everywhere. People said "no cap" when they were being serious or "fire" when something was cool. At first, it wasn't very clear, but now I kind of like it. Social media spreads slang quickly. A single funny video can make a word popular in one day. Teenagers often lead the way, creating and sharing new words, but even adults sometimes join in!

Using slang makes conversations more fun. It’s like having your own secret language with friends. I even taught my older brother what "sus" means, and now he says it too. The slang keeps changing, but it helps us connect. And honestly? That’s pretty fire.

topic: One Family, Two Screens: How Family Time Disappears?
text_body:
Minho sat on the couch, watching a drama on his tablet. His mom entered the room and turned on the TV. "Aren’t you going to watch with me?" she asked. Minho smiled. "I already finished this episode last week," he said. "On Netflix."

When Minho was younger, his family used to watch shows together every evening. But now, everyone watches something different on their own screens. His sister liked YouTube, and his dad only watched live soccer on TV. Minho realized that things had really shifted.

That night, he missed the feeling of watching the same episode together as a family. He thought that if we didn’t all use OTT separately, maybe we would still watch TV as a family. The next day, he suggested a family movie night—with popcorn and just one screen. Everyone agreed. Streaming was great, but sometimes, sharing the moment was even better.

topic: Why Are Countries Heading Back to Space?
text_body:
Have you ever watched The Martian or ET and dreamed about space? These stories feel like science fiction—but space travel is becoming real again. Countries like Korea, the U.S., and India are sending rockets into space. But why?

Some are planning Moon bases. Others are exploring Mars. But space isn’t just about adventure. It helps us here on Earth, too. For example, astronauts on the International Space Station have done experiments to improve medicine, grow plants without soil, and even study Earth’s climate from space.

Their research helps doctors, farmers, and scientists solve real problems. Satellites also guide airplanes, forecast the weather, and connect our phones. In the future, space missions may help us find clean energy or protect Earth from dangerous asteroids. Space is no longer just "out there." It’s part of our life—and our future.

topic: Why Do Countries Go to War?
text_body:
One day, I saw a video of a girl from Ukraine. She was sitting in a train station, holding her cat and waiting to leave her country. Her school was gone, and she didn’t know when she could go home.

I couldn’t stop thinking about her. What did she do to deserve this? Nothing. She was just caught in a war. That night, I asked my dad why countries fight. He said it’s often about power, fear, or not listening to each other. "But people like that girl suffer the most," he said.

Since then, I’ve started to care more about peace. I used to think war only happened in the past, but now I know it’s real—even today. Maybe if more people understand how war affects real lives, they’ll try harder to choose peace over violence.

Last summer, my family went on a camping trip to the mountains. We set up our tent near a small river. Dad made a fire, and we cooked hot dogs for dinner. After dinner, we sat around the fire and told funny stories. I laughed so hard because Dad's story was really silly. At night, I heard strange sounds outside the tent. I was a little scared, but Mom said it was just the wind. The next morning, we went hiking and saw beautiful birds. It was the best trip ever, and I want to go again.

topic: Too Few Babies, Too Many Grandparents? Facing the Future Together
text_body:
Last week in class, our teacher said Korea's birth rate was just 0.78 in 2022. That means, on average, a woman here is expected to have less than one child. I was shocked! Then, she told us that countries like Japan, Italy, and China also have low birthrates.

She explained that this leads to an aging society. That’s when there are more older people than young ones. It sounds scary. If this continues, there might be fewer students, fewer workers, and more people who need care.

I imagined a future where schools would close, robots would help in hospitals, and younger people would have to work more. Some services might get more challenging to keep running, like hospitals or elderly care, because fewer people will pay taxes. What will our lives look like when we grow up? Will we find clever ways to solve it? I really hope so.

topic: Can a Smile Replace a Sentence? The Power of Emojis
text_body:
Have you ever sent just a "smiley face" emoji instead of a full sentence? Emojis are everywhere—in messages, comments, and even group chats. They’re quick, expressive, and can show emotion without using language.

Some people believe emojis can be a substitute for words. A "heart" emoji can mean "I love this," and a "thumbs-up" emoji can mean "OK" or "Nice!" Emojis can even help when people don’t share the same language. But emojis have limits, too. Not everyone interprets them the same way. A "smirking face" emoji might seem friendly to one person but rude to another. And for serious topics, words are often more effective.

So, can emojis replace words? Occasionally—but not always. They help add emotion, but they can’t explain everything. Using emojis and words together might be the most thoughtful way to communicate.

topic: Why Are Short Videos So Popular?
text_body:
Short videos are everywhere. People watch them on TikTok, YouTube Shorts, and Instagram Reels. But why do so many people enjoy them? One reason is that short videos are easy to watch. Even if someone has only a few minutes, they can still enjoy a full video. Many videos are funny or exciting, which makes people want to keep watching more.

Some short videos are educational, too. You can learn how to cook, dance, or solve a math problem in less than a minute. Also, creators use fun music, fast editing, and eye-catching effects. These tricks help the video stay in your mind.

However, watching too many short videos can be a problem. It might become hard to focus on longer tasks, like studying or reading. Although short videos are small, their impact can be both good and bad. They often leave a strong effect on how people spend their time.

topic: Can Virtual Travel Be as Exciting as the Real Thing?
text_body:
Today, we can explore the world in two ways: by traveling or by using technology. Some people have visited famous places like the Taj Mahal through virtual tours. I have walked through the streets of Rome using Google Earth, and it felt exciting in its own way.

Actual travel lets you smell, taste, and feel the world. If you visit a place in person, you will remember how the food tastes or how the air feels. But not everyone can travel far. That’s why virtual tools are so useful—they let people explore without spending money or time.

Still, can virtual experiences truly replace real ones? Maybe not. But they can make learning easier and open doors for more people. The more technology grows, the more ways we have to explore. So, which is better—real or virtual? Maybe the best adventures combine both.

topic: More Than Medals: How the Olympics Bring Us Together
text_body:
Did you know the modern Olympics began in 1896 to bring nations together through sports? From the beginning, the goal wasn’t just about medals—it was about peace. In the 2021 Tokyo Olympics, two high jumpers—from Qatar and one from Italy—tied in the final. Instead of continuing, they decided to share the gold medal. Their friendship showed that sportsmanship can be stronger than competition.

Another powerful moment happened in 2008. A runner from New Zealand helped a fallen opponent from the U.S. during a race. Although they didn’t win, the world praised them for showing true Olympic spirit. People still remember that moment today.

Stories like these inspire the world. The Olympics let people from different countries meet, respect each other, and even help one another. While the games don’t end wars, they show us what peace can look like—fairness, kindness, and hope.

My favorite place is my grandmother's garden. It is small but full of life. In the morning, the flowers open up and reach for the sun. Bees move from one flower to the next, buzzing softly. I like to sit on the old wooden bench under the apple tree. The leaves make soft shadows on the ground. Sometimes a cool breeze blows, and I can smell the roses. Grandmother sings while she waters the plants. Her voice is warm and gentle, like a soft blanket. I feel peaceful and happy there. It is my special place in the whole world.

topic: What Happens in Our Brain When We Learn a Foreign Language?
text_body:
Last year, I started learning English more seriously. At first, it was hard—especially listening and speaking. I kept forgetting words and mixing up grammar. But over time, something strange happened. I could remember new words more quickly, and I was focusing better.

One day, I read that learning a foreign language helps your brain grow stronger. It builds new connections and improves memory and attention. That made sense. I felt like my brain was slowly training, like a muscle at the gym. Now, I have even switched between Korean and English without thinking too much. My teacher says bilingual students often solve problems faster because their brains are more flexible.

Learning English is still challenging, but now I understand what’s happening inside my head. Every time I practice, my brain is growing stronger, making me want to keep going. Learning a language changes more than just how we speak.

topic: Can One Meal Really Help the Earth?
text_body:
Last month, my science teacher gave us a challenge: try eating no meat for one day. At first, I thought, "That’s easy." But I didn’t know it had anything to do with the environment.

Later, I learned that raising animals for food uses a lot of land and water. Even worse, cows release gases that hurt the Earth’s air. I was shocked when I learned that livestock farming causes about 14% of global greenhouse gas emissions—almost the same as all cars and trucks worldwide.

That night, my family ate tofu stir-fry and vegetable pancakes. It tasted better than I expected. My mom said, "We don’t have to stop eating meat, but eating less could help." Now, we try "Meatless Mondays" together. I never thought one dinner could help the planet, but maybe it can. Small choices really do matter.

topic: How Is AI Changing Our Lives?
text_body:
Artificial Intelligence, or AI, is a technology that helps machines "think" and learn like humans. It is already changing the way we live, work, and learn. Many phones use AI to recognize faces or understand voices. Some schools use AI to help students learn faster with personalized lessons.

In hospitals, AI can help doctors find diseases more quickly. Self-driving cars are being tested in some countries, and they use AI to make safe decisions on the road. Even online shopping uses AI to show you items you might prefer.

But AI is not perfect. It can make mistakes, and it can’t feel emotions. People must decide how much they should trust it. AI is a powerful tool, but humans still need to control how it is used. Learning about AI today can help us make wise choices in the future.

One rainy day, I saw a small dog near the bus stop. It was shaking and hungry. I gave it some food and called a local shelter. They said many animals like this dog are abandoned or mistreated. Some are used in labs, others in shows or farms where they suffer.

That night, I searched online and learned about animal rights. I found out some companies test shampoo by putting chemicals in animals’ eyes. Some animals are kept in small cages their whole lives.

Now, I try to buy "cruelty-free" products, which means they were made without testing on animals. I also learned that protecting animals doesn’t mean we stop eating meat or using pets—it means we treat them with care and avoid cruelty. Helping that little dog changed the way I see animals. They may not speak, but they still deserve to be protected.

topic: Preparing for the Future: Can We Stop the Next Pandemic?
text_body:
Can we stop the next pandemic? A pandemic is a disease that spreads across countries or even the entire world. COVID-19 was a pandemic that changed our daily lives. Schools closed, travel stopped, and many people got sick. It showed how fast a new virus can affect everyone.

To stop a future pandemic, experts say we must be better prepared. Countries should share health information quickly so others can respond faster. Scientists also need to study new viruses and make vaccines. During COVID-19, mRNA vaccines were made faster than ever before—this could help save more lives next time.

Hospitals also need enough workers and supplies. If health systems are stronger, they can help more people. We should also teach people how to stay safe and avoid fake news during emergencies. We may not stop all new diseases, but if we prepare now, we can protect more lives in the future.

topic: Solar Power: Can Sunshine Power Our Future?
text_body:
Solar power is one of the fastest-growing energy sources in the world. It works by turning sunlight into electricity using solar panels. These panels can be placed on rooftops or large solar farms.

One famous example is Solar Impulse, a solar-powered airplane that flew around the world without using fuel. In China, a huge solar project called the "Solar Great Wall" is being built to help power cities. Solar energy is clean and renewable. It doesn’t release harmful gases like coal or oil. It also reduces our need for fossil fuels.

However, solar power also has some downsides. Solar panels don’t work as well at night or during cloudy weather. Making and recycling panels also uses energy and materials that can affect the environment. Still, many experts believe solar energy will be an important part of our future. The sun gives us power—if we know how to use it well.

Climate change is becoming more serious every year. Ice caps are melting, the weather is getting more extreme, and many animals are losing their homes. Because of this, many students are joining protests to demand action.

One famous example is Greta Thunberg, a student from Sweden. In 2018, she started a school strike to protest the climate. Millions of students worldwide have joined her movement, asking leaders to protect the Earth.

Some people believe students should protest because they are fighting for their future. If no one speaks up, nothing will change. Protesting can bring attention to important problems. However, others argue that students should stay in school. They believe there are other ways to help, like joining eco-clubs or learning how to live more sustainably. Both sides want to save the planet but have different ideas about how to do it. What would you do?

topic: Can Four Letters Really Describe Who You Are?
text_body:
These days, everyone at Minseo’s school seems to be talking about MBTI. During lunch, students compare types like ENFP, ISTJ, or INTP. Some say it explains why certain friends are loud, quiet, organized, or creative.

Minseo has heard people say things like, "You must be an extrovert—you talk so much!" or "Of course she’s a J. She plans everything." Some teachers have joined in, asking students about their types during group projects. Although MBTI can be fun, Minseo wonders if it’s always accurate. If people are placed into only 16 types, could it leave out parts of who they really are? After all, someone who acts shy today might be outgoing tomorrow.

The test might help people understand themselves better. However, the more students focus on labels, the more they might forget what makes each person different. MBTI is popular—but personalities may be deeper than four letters.

topic: Flying Helpers: How Drones Are Changing Our World
text_body:
Have you ever flown a drone or watched a video from high in the sky? Drones can take amazing photos and videos that were once impossible without helicopters. But today, drones do much more than capture cool views.

In disaster areas, drones search for survivors without risking human lives. In Ukraine, small robots are used to remove landmines safely. At sea, countries like the UK and France use drones to detect dangerous underwater bombs, replacing human divers. Some companies also use drones to inspect bridges and tall buildings, keeping workers out of dangerous situations.

Because drones can fly into risky places, they are now helping humans stay safe. If this technology keeps improving, drones might take over more dangerous jobs in the future. Still, rules must be made for using them safely. Have drones already started to change our lives? In many ways, the answer is "yes."

topic: Fast Fashion: Is Cheap Clothing Worth the Cost?
text_body:
Buying cheap and trendy clothes may seem fun, but fast fashion comes with a hidden cost—and it's not just about money. Behind the low prices, there are serious problems.

Many fast fashion brands use factories where workers are paid very little and work in unsafe conditions. Some workers are even children. They work long hours just so we can wear a shirt once and throw it away. Fast fashion also hurts the Earth. Making clothes fast creates water waste, chemical pollution, and tons of trash. Most of these cheap clothes end up in landfills, where they don’t break down for years. The fashion industry is one of the biggest polluters in the world.

Do we really need ten shirts that fall apart quickly? Or should we buy fewer, better-quality clothes that last longer? Saving money should never come before saving lives—or the planet.

topic: Oops! Was That Rude? Understanding Cultural Differences
text_body:
At an international camp, I met Amir from the Middle East. I handed him something with my left hand one day, and he looked surprised. Later, he told me, "In my culture, we use the right hand for important things." I felt a bit embarrassed, but he smiled and said, "Now you know!"

That wasn’t the only time. My Japanese roommate bowed when we met while I tried to high-five him! And when I joked about hair, my Thai friend seemed uncomfortable—I didn’t know touching someone’s head could be rude there.

I used to think being polite was the same everywhere. But now I know that culture shapes how we act and what we expect. Since then, I’ve learned to ask and listen more. Understanding other cultures doesn’t mean being perfect—it means being curious and respectful.

Water moves in a cycle around the Earth. The sun heats water in rivers and oceans, so it turns into vapor. The vapor goes up into the sky and forms clouds. When the clouds get heavy, rain falls back down to the ground.

Korea has many special holidays during the year. Seollal is the Korean New Year. Families get together and eat a special rice cake soup called tteokguk. Children bow to their grandparents and get money as a gift. Chuseok is another important holiday. It happens in the fall, and families visit their hometowns. They share delicious food like songpyeon, which are small rice cakes. People also remember family members who are no longer alive. Children's Day is on May fifth. Kids get presents, and many families go to parks or amusement parks. These holidays bring families closer together.

topic: Low-sugar & Sugar-free Trend: Are They Really Healthy?
text_body:
Do you choose Coke Zero or the original when you order fast food? Many students today enjoy zero-sugar drinks like Pepsi Zero, Sprite Zero, or even sugar-free milk tea. These products are popular because they taste sweet but contain little or no sugar. Some people believe they’re healthier and better for weight control.

In fact, for people who are trying to avoid sugar, these drinks can be a wise choice. They allow you to enjoy sweet flavors without raising blood sugar levels. But are they really good for your body? Most of these drinks use artificial sweeteners instead of sugar.

While they have fewer calories, some doctors worry they might confuse your body. You might end up wanting more sweets later. Also, "zero sugar" drinks are still high in caffeine or chemicals. So next time you see a sugar-free drink, ask yourself: is it really the healthier choice?

topic: Should Plastic Bags Be Banned Completely?
text_body:
Plastic bags are used almost everywhere—in supermarkets, convenience stores, and even at home. But many people now ask: Should they be banned completely?

Those who support the ban say plastic bags are bad for the environment. They are hard to recycle and often end up in the ocean, where animals can get hurt. Banning them could help reduce pollution and protect nature.

However, others believe that banning plastic bags is not the best idea. Some people still need them for carrying wet or heavy items. Small stores may not have enough money to provide eco-friendly bags. Instead of a complete ban, some countries charge a small fee for plastic bags or encourage people to bring their own. So, should plastic bags be banned completely?

topic: Is Idol Beauty Worth the Price of Health?
text_body:
Have you ever heard of an idol eating only one apple a day to lose weight? In K-pop culture, idols are expected to look perfect on stage. To keep that image, some go on extreme diets—even if it harms their health.

Some say it’s part of the job. Idols are always on camera, and their appearance can affect their career. But the real problem is what this creates: a dangerous beauty standard. Many Korean teenagers feel they must look like idols to be seen as beautiful, even if it’s unhealthy.

Extreme dieting can lead to eating disorders, stress, weak immune systems, and even long-term heart or bone problems. It affects both the body and the mind. This issue isn’t just about idols—it’s about the beauty rules set by the industry. Maybe we should ask if these beauty rules are right instead of just following them.

topic: Sticker Photo Culture: Why Do We Enjoy Taking Sticker Photos?
text_body:
Sticker photo booths have become a big part of Korean teen culture. You can find them almost everywhere—on busy streets, malls, and schools. There are over 3,000 booths across Korea, and their number keeps growing.

Many students enjoy decorating their photos with cute frames, stickers, and fun effects. These photos aren’t just pictures—they show who we are and how we express ourselves. Recently, sticker photos have become popular with foreign tourists, too. Many visitors try them when they come to Korea because it’s a fun and unique way to make memories with friends.

Sticker photos turn small moments into something special. If you’ve taken one before, you probably still remember how fun it was. And if you haven’t, maybe it’s time to try!

topic: Why Are Teenagers Crazy about Bubble Tea?
text_body:
Jisoo had never tried bubble tea until her friend gave her a sip at lunch. The drink was cold and sweet, and the tapioca balls were so fun to chew.

Since that day, she has gone to bubble tea shops every weekend. She tries new flavors and often posts pictures of her drinks online. Her favorite so far has been brown sugar with extra pearls.

Last Saturday, she met some friends at a new shop that had just opened. They laughed, took selfies, and shared sips from each other’s cups. If she hadn’t tried it that first time, she might never have realized how fun it could be. For Jisoo and many other teens, bubble tea isn’t just a drink—it’s part of their weekend adventure.

topic: Eastern vs. Western Superheroes: What’s the Difference?
text_body:
One night, my friend and I argued about who was cooler—Iron Man or Goku. He said Western superheroes are the best because they save the world with cool suits and big explosions. I said Eastern heroes are better because they grow stronger through training and never give up.

We ended up watching both kinds. The hero flew into space to stop a giant robot in one movie. On the other, the character trained every day, made mistakes, and protected his friends from danger. They were so different, but both were exciting.

After that, we realized something. Western heroes often fight huge battles to save everyone. Eastern heroes usually focus on becoming better and protecting the people close to them. In the end, we both agreed: no matter where they’re from, superheroes inspire us to be brave, kind, and strong—even when life gets hard.

topic: Día de los Muertos: How Do Mexicans Celebrate Life Through Memory?
text_body:
One of the most unique festivals in Mexico is Día de los Muertos, or the Day of the Dead. It is celebrated on November 1st and 2nd to honor family members who have passed away. Instead of being sad, this festival is colorful and full of life.

Families build altars at home and decorate them with flowers, candles, food, and photos of loved ones. People also visit graves, bring gifts, and share stories. It’s a time to remember, not to cry.

Skulls made of sugar and skeleton decorations are typical but not scary—they’re symbols of love and memory. Día de los Muertos shows that remembering someone can be beautiful. This festival teaches us that love continues, even after someone is gone.

topic: Different But Together: Living in a Multicultural Society
text_body:
Have you ever watched K-pop stars speak in different languages or seen actors from mixed backgrounds in your favorite shows? Celebrities like NewJeans’ Danielle (Korean-Australian) and BLACKPINK’s Lisa (from Thailand) are great examples of a multicultural world. They show us that different cultures can unite and create something amazing.

A multicultural society means people from many countries and cultures live in the same place. This is happening more in Korea and around the world. We can enjoy different foods, music, and traditions. But sometimes, people may feel left out or face unfair treatment because of their background.

We should be kind, curious, and respectful when living together. Listening to each other’s stories and learning new things helps us grow. Whether in school or society, everyone deserves to feel like they belong. So, how can we live together? By celebrating what makes us different—and what brings us together.

topic: Living in a Virtual World: Welcome to the Metaverse
text_body:
Have you ever played a game where your character walks, talks, and explores a virtual world? That’s part of the metaverse—a digital space where people can use avatars to meet, learn, play, and even work.

The metaverse is more than just a game. People already use it to attend concerts, try on clothes or design buildings. Some schools in Korea use the metaverse for virtual classes and graduation ceremonies. Companies also hold meetings or job interviews inside 3D offices. Supporters say the metaverse can bring people together from anywhere. You can explore museums, practice languages with foreign friends, or even create your own world.

But not everything is perfect. Some worry about losing face-to-face time or becoming too dependent on screens. Still, the metaverse is growing fast—and it’s already becoming a big part of how we learn, play, and connect in the digital age.

My best friend is Jimin. We met on the first day of second grade. I was new at the school, and I did not know anyone. I sat alone at lunch because I was too shy to talk. Then Jimin walked over and sat next to me. He smiled and said hello. He shared his snacks with me, and we started talking about our favorite cartoons. After that day, we did everything together. We walked to school together every morning. We played soccer at recess, and we always picked each other for team projects. One time, I was sick for a whole week. Jimin came to my house every day after school. He brought my homework and told me funny things that happened in class. That made me feel much better. Last year, Jimin moved to a different city because his dad got a new job. I was very sad, but we still talk on the phone every weekend. He tells me about his new school, and I tell him about ours. We are different in many ways. He likes math, but I like reading. He is tall, but I am short. None of that matters, though. Jimin is still my best friend, and he always will be.

You can make a simple volcano at home. You need baking soda, vinegar, a small cup, and a plate. First, put the cup on the plate. Then add two big spoons of baking soda into the cup. You can add a few drops of red food coloring because it looks like real lava. Now pour some vinegar into the cup slowly. The mixture will bubble up and flow over the cup. This happens because the baking soda and vinegar mix together and make a gas called carbon dioxide. The gas makes all the bubbles. It is a fun and safe experiment!

topic: Why Do People Love the Holidays?
text_body:
Have you ever wondered why so many people smile more during the holidays? Christmas is a time when cities glow with colorful lights, and people exchange gifts to show love and care. Many children wait excitedly for Santa, who is said to bring presents to those who have behaved well.

Families gather to eat delicious food, decorate trees, and enjoy music together. Some people like to watch classic Christmas movies or drink hot chocolate by the fire. Others give to those in need because they want to share joy.

Even if it’s cold outside, the warm feeling in people’s hearts makes the season special. That may be why Christmas has been loved by so many for generations—and why it still brings happiness today.

Plants need a few important things to grow. They need sunlight, water, and good soil. A seed is planted in the soil first. Then water helps the seed start to grow. A small root pushes down into the ground, and a tiny stem grows up toward the light. The stem gets taller, and leaves begin to appear. Leaves are important because they take in sunlight. The plant uses sunlight to make its own food. This process is called photosynthesis. Different plants grow at different speeds. Some flowers grow in just a few weeks, but a big tree can take many years.

Animals live in many different places around the world. The place where an animal lives is called its habitat. A habitat gives an animal food, water, and shelter. There are several important types of habitats. Forests are home to many animals. Bears, deer, and squirrels all live in forests. Trees give them food and a safe place to hide. The forest floor is covered with leaves, and many insects live there too. Oceans are another large habitat. Fish, whales, and dolphins swim in the ocean. Some sea animals live near the top of the water because they need air. Others live deep below where it is very dark and cold. Deserts are hot and dry, but some animals still live there. Camels can go a long time without water, so they do well in the desert. Lizards hide under rocks during the day because the sun is too strong. The Arctic is one of the coldest habitats on Earth. Polar bears have thick fur, so they stay warm. Penguins live in large groups and huddle together for warmth. Every animal is special and fits its habitat in an important way. Scientists study habitats to understand how animals survive. Protecting these places is important for all living things.

I think schools should allow small pets in the classroom. There are several good reasons for this idea. First, pets can help students feel calm and happy. Some children feel nervous at school, but petting an animal makes them feel better. Second, taking care of a pet teaches responsibility. Students can learn to feed the pet and keep its home clean. Also, pets make science class more interesting because students can watch and learn about real animals. Some people worry that pets are messy or noisy, but teachers can set clear rules. For example, students can take turns caring for the pet. Classroom pets are a great idea for every school.

When Maya checked the mailbox after school, she discovered a strange envelope. It had no return address, and her name was written in silver ink. Her hands trembled as she opened it. Inside was a single card that read: "Meet me at the old oak tree at sunset."

If you want to build a simple birdhouse, you will need wood, nails, a hammer, and glue. First, cut five pieces of wood that form the walls and floor. Then, attach them together while making sure the structure is stable. Finally, add a small round hole so that birds can enter.

The rain tapped gently on the window, while the old house settled into silence. Each drop that fell seemed to carry a quiet song from the clouds above. She closed her eyes and listened, discovering a strange comfort in the rhythm that nature provided.

Jiwoo had practiced her song for weeks, although she still felt nervous about the talent show. When she walked onto the stage, the bright lights made her freeze. The audience waited in silence. Then she heard her best friend whisper from the front row. "You can do this!" Jiwoo took a deep breath and began to sing. Her voice filled the room, and the crowd started to smile. By the end, everyone was clapping. She realized that courage was not about feeling no fear. It was about singing anyway.

Some people believe that homework should be banned from schools. Students who spend long hours on homework often feel tired and stressed. When children have no free time, they cannot discover hobbies or enjoy being with their families. Although some teachers argue that homework helps students practice, research shows that too much homework does not always improve results. If schools reduced the amount of homework, students could use that time for creative activities and exercise. A balanced approach, which includes short review tasks instead of heavy assignments, would benefit everyone.

Our solar system contains eight planets that orbit the sun. The four inner planets, which are Mercury, Venus, Earth, and Mars, are made of rock. The outer planets are much larger and consist mostly of gas. Scientists continue to discover new facts about these distant worlds.

Many animal species around the world are endangered, which means they could disappear forever. When forests are cut down or oceans become polluted, animals lose the habitats that they need to survive. The giant panda, which lives in the bamboo forests of China, was once close to extinction. However, conservation programs have helped its population grow. People who work to protect endangered animals create safe environments where these creatures can recover. If we compare the results of conservation efforts over the past twenty years, we can see real progress. Although challenges remain, every small action helps protect wildlife.

Every Sunday evening, Mina visited her grandmother, who lived in a small house near the river. The kitchen always smelled of warm soup and fresh bread. While they ate together, her grandmother would share stories about the past. She told tales of a village that no longer existed and people who had traveled far from home. Although the stories were sometimes sad, they made Mina feel connected to her family's history. When her grandmother spoke, her eyes sparkled as if she could see those distant memories clearly. Mina discovered that stories had the power to keep the past alive.

topic: Why did people want to change the church?
text_body:
"Grandpa, why did people want to change the church?" the boy asked, his eyes shining in the firelight. Grandfather smiled and sat back in his chair. "Ah, that is a big question. Let me tell you a story. Long ago, the church was strong, like a great castle. It stood tall and guided people. But over time, it started to break—not the walls, but people's trust."

"Leaders became more focused on power and wealth than on helping people find God. They sold indulgences—papers claiming to forgive sins—for money. Imagine paying to have your soul cleansed! Many felt lost, their faith overshadowed by greed." The boy frowned. "That doesn’t sound right."

"It wasn’t," Grandfather agreed. "A man named Martin Luther stepped forward. He believed people should find God through faith, not by giving money to the church. He said, 'People should read the Bible themselves!' His ideas spread quickly, challenging old beliefs and starting what we call the Reformation."

The boy nodded slowly. "What can we learn from this?" Grandfather's eyes shone. "We should always question things that feel wrong, even if it is scary. And to remember, faith is about love, hope, and truth—not power or riches. Even today, we should seek what is right, not just what is easy."

topic: How should we use AI like ChatGPT?
text_body:
"Hey, Mike, did you know that AI tools like ChatGPT can do some impressive things?" Brian said, leaning on the park bench. "Oh really? Like what?" Mike asked. "Well, it’s very useful for research and writing. It can generate ideas, summarize data, and speed up the work process," Brian explained. "It’s almost like having a super-efficient assistant."

"That sounds great. What else?" Mike inquired. "In customer service, it’s a game-changer," Brian continued. "ChatGPT can quickly handle complex questions, reducing wait times and making replies sound more natural. It even reduces stress for call center workers since they don't have to handle every single inquiry."

"Okay, now I'm interested. What about schools?" Mike asked. "Oh, education's another big one," Brian said. "ChatGPT can spot where students are struggling and give them suitable suggestions. It even looks at trends in how students are performing overall, which helps teachers improve their lessons."

"Sounds like a dream tool," Mike said. "But there’s got to be a downside, right?" "There is," Brian admitted. "If the data it’s trained on is biased, it could give unfair results. Privacy’s a big issue, too—what if personal data gets misused? Plus, AI could take over certain jobs, like editing or customer service. And let’s not forget students submitting AI-written essays, which is basically cheating."

"Yikes," Mike said. "So what’s the solution?" "It’s simple—we just have to use it responsibly," Brian replied. "AI should be developed ethically and applied in a fair way. It can do amazing things, like improving healthcare or sustainability, but we need to make sure we control the risks."

If you want to create a simple computer game, follow these steps. First, choose a programming language that is easy to learn, such as Scratch or Python. When you open the program, start by designing your main character, which will be a small sprite on the screen. Next, write code that tells the character how to move when you press the arrow keys. Although it seems complicated, the basic commands are simple to understand. Test your game often and compare the results each time to discover what works best. After you fix any problems, share your finished game with friends who enjoy playing.

topic: How has the way we think changed over time?
text_body:
Once a day, when James was suddenly curious about how people behaved differently now, James looked up at his father. "Dad, why do people think so differently now compared to the past?"

His father smiled. "That's a great question, son. Our way of thinking has changed a lot over time. Long ago, people believed in animism, thinking everything—rivers, trees, even the wind—had a spirit. It was their way of explaining the world. Then, as societies grew, people developed religious doctrines to create order and moral rules. Early religions worshipped multiple gods, like Mars, the God of war, and Venus, the goddess of love. Later, Christianity, Judaism, and Islam introduced the idea of one powerful God, shaping laws and values."

James nodded. "So religion helped people understand life?" "Exactly," his father continued. "Then came the Scientific Revolution, when thinkers like Francis Bacon encouraged observation, experimentation, and evidence instead of relying only on faith. This led to advances in physics, biology, and chemistry. After that, the Enlightenment encouraged reason, freedom, and questioning old ideas."

James thought for a moment. "And what about today?" "Now, in the Information Age, we have access to endless knowledge but also misinformation. That's why we must think critically." James smiled. "So learning and questioning things help us grow?" His father patted his shoulder. "That's right, son. Never stop asking questions—that's how we move forward."

When Seoho's family moved to a new city, everything felt unfamiliar. The streets were wider, the buildings were taller, and the school was enormous compared to his old one. On his first day, he sat alone at lunch while other students talked and laughed around him. He missed his old friends, who always saved him a seat. After a few days, a boy named Daniel noticed Seoho sitting by himself. Daniel walked over and asked if he wanted to join a game of basketball. Although Seoho was not very good at basketball, he agreed to play. The other kids did not mind that he missed most of his shots. They cheered for him when he finally scored a point. Slowly, Seoho began to discover that making friends in a new place was possible. He learned that being brave enough to say yes was the first step. By the end of the month, Seoho had a small group of friends who made the new city feel like home. He still missed his old town sometimes, but he understood that change could bring good things if he gave it a chance.

topic: Can computers really learn like humans?
text_body:
People thought only humans could learn. Well, how could a machine think or understand? But today, computers can learn in ways similar to humans, thanks to something called machine learning.

Imagine you want to teach a computer to recognize spam emails. At first, the computer doesn’t know what spam is, just like a child learning something new. So, we start by giving it examples. We show it many emails, some marked as "spam" and others as "not spam." This is like teaching a kid by showing them examples—"This is an apple, and this is not."

Next, the computer looks for patterns. It notices that spam emails often have certain words, like "free" or "prize," or they come from unknown senders. Over time, it starts making guesses, like when a child begins to recognize apples because they’ve seen enough of them. The computer then practices. We test it with new emails to see if it guesses correctly. If it’s wrong, it adjusts to do better. With enough practice, it becomes really good at spotting spam, even in emails it has never seen before.

Computers learn by studying examples, finding patterns, and improving over time. This is similar to how people learn. Machine learning is used in many areas. It can recommend movies or help doctors detect diseases. This technology offers many new opportunities.

topic: How does social media affect our lives and friendships?
text_body:
Emma scrolled through her phone, laughing at a friend’s post. A notification popped up—another like on her latest picture. She felt happy, but when she looked up, her brother was waiting for her to answer his question. "Did you hear me?" he asked. She hadn’t.

Social media connects people in once-impossible ways. Friends can stay in touch worldwide, share experiences instantly, and express themselves creatively. It allows people to find support, discover new interests, and stay informed. However, social media also has drawbacks. It can create pressure to present a perfect life, leading to comparisons that make people feel insecure. Scrolling endlessly can take time away from real-life relationships, making face-to-face conversations less frequent.

Friendships on social media can be both strengthened and weakened. While it helps people maintain connections, misunderstandings can arise because online communication lacks tone and body language. Some may feel left out when they see friends spending time together without them.

Too much social media use can also lead to loneliness if virtual interactions replace real ones. Emma realized she needed balance. She put her phone down and smiled at her brother. "Sorry, what were you saying?" Social media is powerful, but real-life moments matter most.

topic: How did the green revolution change the world?
text_body:
A long time ago, many people in the world didn’t have enough food. Families worried about their next meal, and farmers worked hard but couldn’t grow enough crops. Hunger was everywhere, and it seemed like no one could stop it.

Then, something amazing happened. In the 1940s, a scientist named Norman Borlaug and his team started experimenting with wheat plants in Mexico. They asked, "What if we could make plants that grow more food and resist diseases?" After years of work, they created a new kind of wheat. This wheat was stronger, grew faster, and produced more grain. Farmers started using it, and their fields became full of golden crops.

The idea spread to other countries, like India and Pakistan. Farmers also started using new seeds, better fertilizers, and irrigation systems to water their crops. Suddenly, places where people used to go hungry had enough food to eat. This big change was called the Green Revolution, and it saved millions of lives.

But there were challenges, too. Using chemicals hurt the environment, and some farmers couldn’t afford the new seeds and tools. People are working on ways to grow food that protects the Earth and helps everyone. The Green Revolution showed us what we can do when we work together to solve big problems.

topic: Why should we save the ocean, and what can we do?
text_body:
Imagine a world without the ocean—no crashing waves, no seafood, and no fresh air to breathe. Covering 71% of the Earth, the sea is essential for life. It controls the climate, provides oxygen, and is home to countless marine species. It also supports industries like aquaculture and tourism, helping millions of people earn a living. However, the ocean is in danger due to contamination, overharvesting, and environmental changes. To protect our planet, we must take action now.

Small actions can make a big difference in protecting the ocean. Reducing plastic waste by using reusable bags and bottles helps prevent contamination. Eating ethical seafood supports healthy fish populations. Conserving water and reducing carbon footprints—driving less and saving energy—help slow environmental changes, affecting ocean temperatures and coral reefs. Supporting ocean conservation groups and spreading awareness also encourages more considerable environmental efforts.

To save the ocean, we must take several steps. Creating protected areas helps marine life recover. Responsible aquaculture, reducing contamination, and using biodegradable products are crucial. Participating in beach cleanups and reducing greenhouse gas emissions by supporting clean energy can slow environmental changes. Educating people about ocean conservation and joining community efforts can make a big impact.

The ocean affects our air, food, and climate. It also provides recreation and relaxation. We can ensure a healthy ocean for future generations by taking action now. Protecting the ocean is not just an environmental concern—it is essential for the well-being of all living things.

A computer is a machine that follows instructions to process information. When you type on a keyboard, the computer receives signals and converts them into data. The processor, which is often called the brain of the computer, handles millions of calculations each second. It compares and sorts information at incredible speed. The results appear on your screen almost instantly. Although computers seem very smart, they can only do what humans program them to do. Every app and game that you enjoy was created by a person who wrote special code.

topic: How can we take care of our minds and feel happier?
text_body:
Have you ever questioned what it takes to feel truly happy and live a fulfilling life? Maintaining good mental health is essential for overall well-being. It affects our emotions, thoughts, and behaviors, helping us manage stress, build relationships, and make wise decisions.

Good mental health improves our quality of life by allowing us to enjoy life’s experiences and find purpose. It also affects physical health, as poor mental well-being can lead to issues like heart disease and obesity. Additionally, it helps us function better daily, handle stress, and contribute positively to our communities. It increases resilience, strengthens relationships, and improves decision-making skills.

On the other hand, poor mental health can make it difficult to enjoy and manage daily life. Conditions like depression and anxiety are common indicators. Depression causes lasting sadness, loss of interest in activities, and changes in eating and sleeping habits. Anxiety brings strong fear, panic, and physical signs like a fast heartbeat and sweating. People can manage these conditions with therapy, medication, and healthy lifestyle changes.

To stay mentally healthy, we should exercise, eat well, get good sleep, and spend time with others. Relaxing and being mindful can help lower stress. Taking care of ourselves and asking for help when needed are important. These habits can make life happier and more fulfilling.

topic: How do we learn new things in different ways?
text_body:
How do we transform from being unaware of something to fully understanding it? Learning happens in different ways, and it involves three key steps: studying, learning, and knowing. These stages work together to help us gain confidence, foundation, application, interpretation, and retention of knowledge and skills.

Studying is like preparing for a journey. It involves gathering facts, reading, watching videos, and listening to lectures. For example, studying would include learning about guitar parts and reading music if you're learning to play the guitar. However, studying alone does not mean you fully understand something—it just provides the foundation.

Learning happens when you start applying what you've studied. It’s like taking a trip—you make mistakes, adjust, and improve. In the guitar example, application means actually playing, hearing the sounds, and practicing until you get better. Mistakes are a natural part of learning.

Knowing is when you can use your skills confidently. It’s like reaching your destination—you no longer need to think hard about what to do. A guitarist who knows how to play can perform smoothly and with confidence.

Some people believe learning happens through experience, like touching something hot and knowing not to do it again. Others see it as an active process of building knowledge through interpretation and improving retention based on past experiences. In the end, studying, learning, and knowing are all connected in an ongoing journey that never truly ends!

topic: What can we do to keep nature safe and healthy?
text_body:
As Mia hiked through the forest, she noticed birds singing, a deer drinking from a clear stream, and the fresh scent of pine in the air. But she also saw litter along the trail and trees with carved initials. It made her wonder—how can we keep nature safe and healthy?

Protecting nature starts with small, everyday actions. One of the simplest ways is to reduce waste by recycling, using reusable items, and properly disposing of trash. Plastic pollution harms wildlife and ecosystems, so cutting back on single-use plastics makes a big difference. Another important step is conserving natural resources. Saving water, using less electricity, and supporting sustainable products help reduce environmental harm. Planting trees and growing native plants also improve air quality and provide shelter for animals.

Respecting wildlife is just as important. Avoid disturbing animals in their habitats, stay on marked trails while hiking, and never feed wild creatures, as it can disrupt their natural behaviors. Supporting conservation efforts—whether through volunteering, donating, or spreading awareness—also plays a key role in keeping ecosystems healthy.

We depend on nature for the air we breathe, the water we drink, and the environment we live in. Our everyday choices have a lasting impact. By acting wisely now, we safeguard the planet for the generations that follow. No action is too small when it comes to protecting our future.

topic: What is big data, and how does it make our lives better?
text_body:
Sophia tapped her phone to order a ride, and within seconds, an app found the nearest driver. Later, she streamed her favorite music, noticing how the app suggested songs she might like. She didn’t realize it, but behind these conveniences was something powerful—Big Data.

Big Data refers to the vast amounts of information generated every second from phones, computers, and sensors. This data is collected, analyzed, and used to improve services, make predictions, and solve problems. Companies use Big Data to personalize experiences, like recommending movies on streaming platforms or showing relevant ads.

In healthcare, Big Data helps doctors detect diseases earlier and predict outbreaks. Businesses use it to track trends and improve customer service. Even traffic apps analyze data from millions of users to suggest the fastest routes. Governments rely on Big Data to improve city planning, monitor environmental changes, and respond to emergencies.

While Big Data brings many benefits, it also raises concerns about privacy and security. Responsible data use is essential to balance innovation with protection. Sophia arrived at her destination smoothly, thanks to Big Data. Though she didn’t see it working, it quietly made her life easier, shaping the world in more intelligent, more efficient ways.

topic: How did COVID-19 change the way we learn?
text_body:
As Emma logged into her virtual classroom, she missed her classmates' chatter and her school's familiar sights. Before COVID-19, learning meant sitting in a classroom, but everything had changed. The COVID-19 pandemic transformed education worldwide, forcing students and teachers to adapt quickly. Schools closed, and online learning became the new normal. Students attended virtual classes, submitted assignments digitally, and used video calls instead of in-person discussions. While technology helped keep learning going, many faced challenges—unstable internet, lack of focus at home, and fewer social interactions.

However, the pandemic also led to new ways of learning. Digital tools like interactive apps, recorded lessons, and online tutoring became more common. Many students gained independence, learning to manage their time and study independently. Teachers adapted using creative methods, such as virtual labs and digital group projects.

Even after schools reopened, the effects remained. Blended learning, which combines online learning and in-person education, has become more popular. Schools also focused more on mental health, recognizing the emotional toll of isolation and uncertainty. The pandemic also changed teaching methods. Instead of traditional classroom instruction, teachers had to find creative ways to keep students engaged online. Interactive lessons, recorded lectures, and online quizzes became more common. Students also had to develop stronger self-discipline and time management skills since they were learning from home.
